---
title: "implementation"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

# Implementation of graph, index, and calculation relationships.

- An `indexRange` manages the index content of one dimension of one variable.

    - This is implemented as a class hierarchy, with subclasses for scalar, sequence (e.g., `2:10`), matrix (i.e., arbitrary).

- An `indexRule` manages the determination of LHS indices from RHS indices for one or more `indexRange`s.  (Sometimes multiple index dimensions need to be solved jointly.)

    - Kinds of `indexRule`s include 'all', 'arbitrary', 'block', and 'constant'.
    - Inputs and outputs to application of an `indexRule` are `indexRange`s.
    - Inputs that are out of range do not produce output
    - Includes checks for going out of range
    - By reversing the order of a declaration, we can create both parent and child rules
    - Various possibilities for either input or output include no indexing, constant scalar, constant vector, arbitrary, and block. 

- An `originalIndexingRule` manages the determination of nodeFunction indices from actual index values, e.g., with a declaration such as `y[i+1] <- x[i]`, determining that calculation for `y[2]` is handled with `i=1`.

    - This is handled by the `indexRule` functionality by making dummy declarations such as `w[i,j] <- y[i+j]` if the focal declaration is, say, `y[i+j] <- x[i] + x[j]`.

- A `varRange` manages a set of `indexRange`s representing all dimensions of a variable.

    - A `varRange` also keeps track of how each `indexRange` is crossed or combined with others to represent the set of variable elements correctly.
    - Ultimately we may want to be able to [collapse multiple `varRange`s to a single `varRange`](combining_ranges.html) when possible.

- A `graphRule` manages the set of `indexRule`s needed to determine all output indices from all input indices for one set of edges created by one of the input variables and the output variable of a single declaration.

    - Inputs and outputs to a `graphRule` are `varRange`s.
    - `graphRule` also can contain `indexConstraint`s that don't map to an output index but need to be enforced, such as `y[i] <- x[2]`.
    - Dynamic indexing is handled similarly to as in current nimble -- all possible dependencies are included in the rule.
    
- A `varRules` object manages the set of rules (`graphRule`s, various kinds of `nodeRule`s) for the same variable.

- A `nodeRule` manages a single set of nodes from a declaration that are equivalent in some sense. 

    - `nodeRule` is base class for `declRule`, `calcRule`, and `RHSrule`
    - Has external and internal rules for handling `i` and `1:3` in `y[i,1:3]`. Rules are `graphRule`s (though without the notion of two sides).
    - Input is a `varRange`.
    - Output is a `nodeRange`.
    - Indexing is over nodes (see comments below regarding `nodeRange`).

- A `nodeRange` compactly represents a set of equivalent nodes

    - E.g., `y[i, 1:3] for i in 1:500` or `y[i, j] for i in 1:5, j in 1:20`
    - A subclass of `varRange` that knows which `indexRanges` are external (`i` in `y[i,1:3]`) and internal (`1:3` in `y[i,1:3]`.
    - Various API calls (e.g., `getNodes`, `getDependencies`) will work with either `varRange`s or `nodeRange`s transparently to the user.

` A `declRule` manages information from a single declaration
    - Generates the nodeFunction, `calcFun`, as function of a vector of scalar-valued index arguments (e.g., `calcFun(idx_vector)`)
    - Contains `originalIndexingRule` with indexing information needed for calculations to pass along to related `calcRule`s.
    - Created by initial pass through model, with LHS from the declarations
    - Notion of "equivalence" in terms of sort order, type, etc. is pushed down into `calcRule`s.

` A `rhsRule` manages information about RHS only nodes
    - Produced from original `rhsRule`s generated from all RHS terms in a declaration and subsequent modification of those rules based on all or portions of the RHS appearing in a different LHS rule (a `calcRule`) via `exclude()`.

- A `calcRule` manages a single `varRange` that can be calculated jointly.
    - Inherits from the `nodeRule` base class as a `calcRule` generally represents subsets of nodes containing elements of all the same type (e.g., `top`, `end`).
    - An individual `calcRule` is produced from the elements in a declaration that are tied in terms of their sort order and (are all of the same type).
    - Includes parent/child links.
    - This avoids having to store information in `variableStore`s and can use all of our tools for working with `indexRange`s and `varRange`s.
    - When passed a `varRange` (or a `nodeRange`) it returns a `calcRange` (or nothing if there is no overlap) that can be used for calculation.
    - Produced from original `calcRule` generated by each `declRule` and subsequent fracturing of those rules (using `fracture()`) based on walking down the graph.  (Noting that `theta[i] ~ dnorm(theta0[i], 1)` are not necessarily all latent or all top nodes, as it depends on how `theta0[i]` are declared; this involve splitting an original `calcRule` when graph processing is done.)
       - everything on LHS starts out as both `top` and `end`
       - `top` found by walking down the graph (from known `top` [those with only constant RHS] and `RHSonly`), fracturing, and tagging what has stoch parents; the resulting `calcRule`s that don't have stoch parents are `top`
       - `end` found by starting with final `calcRule`s with no dependents. Then also walk up from each stoch end rule and anything found along the way is removed from being `end`
       - `latent` found by what is not top or end
    - `sortID` (topological sorting) determined by recursion based on walking the final set of `calcRules` (which have parent/child information) bottom-up (so that all data nodes are set to be tied, given data nodes generally more numerous than parameter nodes)
       - `sortID` can be a vector in state-space type cases.

- A `calcRange` manages the indexing for a single calculation for a variable

    - Keeps track of declaration (via `declRule`, including associated nodeFunction, `calcFun`), indexing (using `originalIndexRange`s) (and nodeFunction version to be used).
    - Index values are based on original declaration context, e.g., a declaration of `y[i+1] ~ dnorm(x[i], 1)` with `i=1:5` has `calcRange` indexing of `1:5` since nodeFunctions use `i` directly. 
    - Need to discuss how to provide vectorized calculations.
        - Currently `calcRange` has a calculation function that loops over ranges of original indexes and calls `declRule` scalar-index-valued nodeFunction (`calcFun`).
        - Currently unclear how the compilation of this would work.
    - Includes `sortID` so that `calcRange` can be calculated in correct order relative to other `calcRange`s.

- A `dataRule` manages information about  data (or non-data) elements.
    - Application of such a rule produces a `varRange`.


- [Needed? Not done] A `copyRule` manages the determination of copying for a variable

    - presumably trivial but a bit fuzzy at the moment
    - Input is a `varRange`?
    - Output is a `varRange`.
    - Perhaps all it needs to do is check for out of bounds and expand missing indexes?

- (Deprecated?) A `variableStore` manages array-like information for variables (see below for alternative idea of using `varTypeRule`s)

    - Stores information such as sort ID, isData, node types
    - Could be integer, logical, or character-valued
    - Should efficiently handle case where all values are the same
    - Ultimately may want to handle additional structure, such as blocks of equal values
       - Could have sets of blocks, with remaining values a "discontinuous block" if all the same.
    - Ultimately may want to handle simple sequential values (e.g., row-major or column-major in a matrix) as this could be useful for sort ID values in state-space/time-series contexts.
    - Should we have `variableStore`s that map back to `calcRule`s and `nodeRule`s? This might avoid need to store something like node type if all node types for a given `nodeRule` are the same. Also avoids need for variable-level sort ID unless there is an ordering with a given `calcRule` (since could just look up sort ID of the `calcRule`).


- Dynamic indexing still needs consideration.

    - Currently we introduce a hidden 'unknownIndex' lifted node that allows for our standard dependency processing.
    - Possibly handle things similarly to current approach: have one `graphRule` with `varRange` of the 'unknownIndex' as the LHS and another `graphRule` with `varRange` of the 'unknownIndex' as the RHS.
